card.intuition: Bundlers (like Webpack, Vite, Rollup) take hundreds of messy development files and combine them into one or two highly optimized files that the browser can load quickly.
card.analogy: A professional mover: you have a messy house with thousands of random items. The mover (Bundler) comes in, organizes everything into boxes by room (graph traversal), labels them, and loads them into a single truck (the Bundle) for the journey.
when_to_use: Compiling modern JS/TS for browsers that don't support new features yet
Implementing Hot Module Replacement (HMR) for faster development
Merging CSS, Images, and JS into a single deployment pipeline
gotchas: Modern bundlers like Vite use 'Module Federation' or 'No-Bundle' dev servers to speed up the loop by not bundling during development at all
Excessive 'Chunking' can actually slow down a site by causing too many parallel network requests

exercise.prompt: Identify the two main stages of a modern bundler.
exercise.starter_code:
stages = [
    "1. Dependency ________: Finding all the files that are imported",
    "2. Code __________: Merging and minifying them into a single file"
]
hints: First you 'Resolve' the map, then you 'Generate' the output
exercise.test_code:
assert "Reso" in stages[0] or "Graph" in stages[0]
assert "Gen" in stages[1] or "Bundle" in stages[1]
print("PASS")
